title: Writing the PT Procedure: Required Content
narrationScript: A penetrant procedure is not an essay. It is a document with a fixed skeleton, and in this lesson we write it section by section. The governing practice requires every penetrant examination to run to a written procedure, approved by a certified Level three before it reaches the floor. The foreword states the scope and the reference documents. What parts the procedure covers, which documents it implements, and which document wins when they conflict. The product section identifies the hardware. Part number, alloy, manufacturing stage, and a drawing of the area to be tested, with exclusions masked. Test conditions name the qualified materials by their A M S twenty six forty four classification, the equipment, the temperature band, and the preparation for testing. The detailed instructions are the heart. Every step carries an action, a numeric window, a clock, and a check, so two inspectors get the same result. Finally, the procedure defines what is recorded, how indications are classified, and exactly what the report contains, down to the procedure revision and the inspector's signature.

sections.heading: The Procedure Is a Document with a Fixed Skeleton
sections.body:
ASTM E1417 requires every penetrant examination to run to a **written procedure** applicable to the specific part or family of parts — and that procedure carries the approval of a certified **PT Level III** before it reaches the floor. Drafting, though, is rarely done in an office vacuum: it takes someone who knows both the governing documents and the bench, which is why senior Level II inspectors routinely build the draft the Level III approves. Knowing the document is part of knowing the method.

A workable PT procedure is built from six working sections, and each answers exactly one question for the person holding the part:

- **Foreword** — scope and reference documents: *does this procedure apply to this job?*
- **Product** — description or drawing: *what hardware, what stage, what surfaces?*
- **Test conditions** — materials, equipment, environment, preparation: *with what, under what conditions?*
- **Detailed instructions** — the step-by-step: *exactly how?*
- **Recording and classifying** — *what do I do with what I find?*
- **Reporting** — *how are results communicated and kept?*

Write those six sections completely and a qualified stranger can run your inspection and get your result. That is the entire test of a procedure.

sections.caption: Six sections, six questions — the working skeleton of a written PT procedure, approved by a certified Level III before first use.

sections.heading: The Foreword: Scope, References, and Precedence
sections.body:
The foreword is the gatekeeper. Its **scope statement** says what the procedure covers — the part families, alloys, manufacturing stage, and penetrant system — and, just as deliberately, what it does not. If the job in front of you falls outside the scope, the procedure does not apply, and adapting it on the fly is an unauthorized deviation no matter how reasonable the change feels.

Next come the **reference documents**: every document the procedure implements or leans on, listed by number — the governing practice (ASTM E1417, or ASME Section V, Article 6, as the contract invokes), SAE AMS 2644 for qualified penetrant materials, the source of the acceptance criteria, and any customer specification flowed down by the purchase order. The foreword also states which revision of each applies — a fixed revision, or the revision in effect per the contract — because requirements move when editions change.

Finally, the foreword settles conflicts before they happen with an **order of precedence**: typically the purchase order or contract first, then the customer specification, then this procedure and the industry practice beneath it. One direction is never negotiable — the procedure may be *more* conservative than the documents it implements, never less.

sections.heading: The Product Section: What Hardware, What Stage, What Surfaces
sections.body:
The product section tells the inspector exactly what is on the bench. Three things, with no guessing:

- **Identification.** Part number or part family, material and alloy, and the processing condition that matters to PT — machined, ground, welded, heat treated — plus any coating that must come off before the test, because penetrant cannot enter a flaw sealed under paint or plating.
- **Stage in the sequence.** PT is positioned *after* the operations that can create or expose surface flaws and *before* the operations that can close or cover them. A typical aerospace callout: after final machining and etch, before shot peening, anodize, paint, or plating. The procedure states the stage; the router does not get to decide.
- **Area to be tested.** A description — better, a drawing — of the surfaces the examination covers: 100 % of accessible surfaces, or defined zones keyed to drawing features. Excluded areas are listed with their reason and their masking instructions, because a sealed cavity or a pressed-in bushing that swallows penetrant will bleed for hours and paint the part with false indications.

sections.caption: The product section at work — part identification, manufacturing stage, and the area to be tested, with exclusions stated and masked rather than assumed.

sections.heading: Test Conditions and Preparation for Testing
sections.body:
The test-conditions section arms the inspector before step one:

- **Materials.** The penetrant system named by its AMS 2644 classification — Type, Method, sensitivity Level, developer Form, remover class — plus the manufacturer and product designation from the Qualified Products List. The penetrant and its emulsifier must come from the same manufacturer's qualified family; penetrant families are never intermixed.
- **Equipment.** Stationary processing line or portable kit, UV-A sources, radiometer and visible-light meter, timers, and pressure and temperature gauges — each with its calibration requirement.
- **Environment.** The standard processing band: part and materials between 40 and 125 °F (4 and 52 °C). Outside that band the technique is nonstandard and must be separately qualified and approved before anyone uses it.
- **Preparation for testing.** The pre-cleaning methods authorized for this alloy and the soils expected; the drying time after cleaning, so solvents and water evaporate out of the very flaws you are hunting; whether an etch is required to reopen flaw mouths after metal-smearing operations such as grinding, blasting, or buffing; and the masking already called out by the product section.

sections.heading: Detailed Instructions: Where the Procedure Earns Its Keep
sections.body:
This is the heart of the document — the section the inspector works from, written for the specific Type and Method so the wrong branch cannot be followed. A Method D procedure carries the pre-rinse, remover concentration, and contact time; a Method C procedure carries the wipe sequence — clean, dry, lint-free wipes first, then a solvent-dampened wipe, with solvent never flushed onto the part.

Every step is written to the same four-part anatomy:

- **Action and method** — immerse, spray, flow, dust; with what equipment.
- **Window** — the numbers, with units and tolerances: temperatures, pressures, concentrations, distances.
- **Clock** — when timing starts and stops, with the minimum and the maximum.
- **Check** — what is verified before the next step begins.

"Rinse the part thoroughly" is a hope, not an instruction. "Spray rinse with water at 50–100 °F and 40 psi maximum, under UV-A illumination, until fluorescent background is removed" is an instruction — it has a window and a check. The test of the whole section: two qualified inspectors, same part, same procedure, same result, zero questions asked.

sections.caption: What the detailed-instructions section pins down at each step — example windows consistent with ASTM E1417. The governing procedure always states its own.
rows: Penetrant application
Method (immerse, spray, brush, flow), coverage, temperature at application
Part and materials within 40–125 °F (4–52 °C)
Penetrant dwell
Minimum and maximum dwell; action if penetrant dries
Minimum 10 min; the surface stays wet through dwell — dried penetrant means clean and reprocess
Excess removal
Water temperature and pressure, remover concentration and contact time, background check
Spray rinse 50–100 °F (10–38 °C) at 40 psi (275 kPa) max; hydrophilic remover ≤35 % immersion, ≤5 % spray
Drying
Oven type, maximum part surface temperature, minimum effective time
Part surface not above 160 °F (71 °C); minimum time that actually dries the part
Developer
Form, application method, film target, part condition at application
Form a (dry) with Type I only; forms d/e in thin passes on a completely dry part
Development time
When the clock starts, minimum and maximum
Minimum 10 min; maximum 4 h dry, 2 h aqueous, 1 h nonaqueous
Examination
Light type and minimum intensity, ambient limit, adaptation time
UV-A ≥1,000 µW/cm² at the surface, ambient ≤2 fc, ≥1 min dark adaptation; or ≥100 fc white light for Type II

sections.heading: Recording, Classifying, and Reporting the Results
sections.body:
The last two sections decide what happens to what you find.

**Recording and classifying.** The procedure states which indications are recorded — commonly every rejectable indication, plus relevant indications above a stated size — and how locations are captured: a marked sketch, a photograph, or coordinates from a drawing datum. It defines the classification language the evaluator applies, or points to the exact paragraph of the acceptance document that does: **relevant** versus nonrelevant, **linear** — length more than three times the width — versus **rounded**, and how aligned indications are treated. It also restricts marking media, because a hard stamp or etch in the wrong place is its own defect.

**Reporting.** The report is the part's voice in the file, so the procedure lists its minimum content: part identification and quantity; procedure number **and revision**; materials used with manufacturer, designation, and batch; the parameters actually run; results and disposition for each item; the acceptance criteria invoked; and the inspector's name, certification level, signature or stamp, and date — with distribution and retention per the contract and quality system. The bar it must clear is simple: a stranger, years later, can establish exactly what was done, to what requirement, with what outcome.

figures.title: Anatomy of a written PT procedure
Product section: defining the area to be tested

interactive.title: Procedure Section Builder
interactive.intro: A Level III hands you eight requirement statements for a new Method D procedure. File each one in the section of the document where it belongs.

items.label: Applies to machined 7075 aluminum fittings after final machining, before anodize
items.why: Applicability — which hardware and which manufacturing stage — is the foreword's scope statement, expanded by the product section's part description.

items.label: In any conflict, the purchase order governs over this procedure
items.why: That is the order of precedence, and it lives in the foreword with the reference documents so conflicts are settled before work starts.

items.label: Excluded: the pressed-in bushing bore, masked to prevent penetrant entrapment
items.why: Exclusions from the area to be tested belong in the product section, stated with their reason and masking instructions — never assumed.

items.label: Penetrant: Type I, Method D, Sensitivity Level 3, listed on the QPL to AMS 2644
items.why: Naming the qualified materials by AMS 2644 classification, manufacturer, and designation is test-conditions content — it arms the inspector before step one.

items.label: Part and materials shall be between 40 and 125 °F throughout processing
items.why: The standard temperature band is a test condition; outside it the technique is nonstandard and needs separate qualification and approval.

items.label: Spray rinse with 50–100 °F water at 40 psi maximum until background is removed
items.why: A step with an action, a numeric window, and a check is exactly what the detailed-instructions section is made of.

items.label: An indication whose length is more than three times its width is classified linear
items.why: Classification definitions — linear versus rounded, relevant versus nonrelevant — belong with recording and classifying so two evaluators classify identically.

items.label: The report lists penetrant batch numbers, procedure revision, and the inspector's name and level
items.why: Minimum report content is defined by the reporting section — it is what lets an auditor reconstruct the inspection years later.

checkQuestions.question: A traveler sends you a cast steel pump housing, but your PT procedure's foreword states it covers machined aluminum and titanium parts only. What does the scope statement tell you to do?
options: Proceed — the detailed instructions are the only binding part of a procedure
Stop — the procedure does not apply, and inspecting the casting to it is an unauthorized deviation
Proceed, but double the penetrant dwell to compensate for the different material
Proceed if the casting is about the same size as the parts the procedure lists
checkQuestions.explanation: The scope defines exactly what the procedure applies to, and using it outside that boundary is a deviation no one authorized. The job needs a procedure whose scope covers it — or a revision approved through the same Level III path.

checkQuestions.question: Which content belongs in the product section of a written PT procedure?
options: A description or drawing of the part identifying the area to be tested, including excluded or masked regions
The calibration schedule for the shop's radiometers and light meters
The order of precedence among the reference documents
The minimum and maximum development times
checkQuestions.explanation: The product section identifies the hardware and the surfaces the examination covers — including what is excluded and why. Equipment and calibration sit in test conditions, precedence in the foreword, and development times in the detailed instructions.

checkQuestions.question: Which line is written at the level of detail the detailed-instructions section requires?
options: Remove excess penetrant carefully
Rinse until the part looks clean
Wash the part with clean water
Spray rinse with 50–100 °F water at 40 psi maximum, under UV-A, until fluorescent background is removed
checkQuestions.explanation: An adequate instruction carries an action and method, a numeric window with units, and a verification check. The other three leave the deciding to the inspector, which is exactly what a procedure exists to prevent.

checkQuestions.question: During evaluation, an indication measures 6 mm long and 1.5 mm wide. Using the classification convention a procedure carries into its recording section, it is:
options: Nonrelevant, because it is shorter than 10 mm
Rounded, because any indication under 10 mm is treated as rounded
Linear, because its length is more than three times its width
False, because true indications are never that wide
checkQuestions.explanation: The common convention — used by acceptance standards such as ASME's penetrant acceptance appendix — classifies an indication as linear when its length is more than three times its width: here 6 mm is four times 1.5 mm. The procedure writes the definition down so every evaluator classifies the same way.

checkQuestions.question: Three years from now an auditor pulls one of your PT reports. Which entry lets them establish exactly which requirements the part was inspected and accepted to?
options: The procedure number alone — revisions never change requirements
The development time used that day
The procedure number and revision, plus the acceptance criteria invoked
The developer form, since sensitivity depends on it
checkQuestions.explanation: Requirements live in a specific revision of the procedure and in the acceptance document it invokes, so the report must capture both. A bare procedure number is not enough — content moves when revisions change.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline (test procedures: foreword, product, test conditions, detailed instructions, recording and reporting of results)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing (written procedure requirement and process parameter limits)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (written procedure requirements; essential and nonessential variables)
SAE AMS 2644, Inspection Material, Penetrant (qualified materials cited by the procedure)
